The Mango-Eating Frontend Dev (20): MongoDB Update Operators

After writing a ton of Mongoose, it hit me that I'd skipped over MongoDB's Update Operators — and honestly that part matters a lot. So here's a make-up chapter covering Update Operators in MongoDB, including the handy $inc operator for incrementing and decrementing fields.
Mongoose Has Middleware Too? Hooking Into save with pre and post
The Frontend Engineer Forced to Eat Mango

Hold on — Mongoose has Middleware too! Middleware is basically a middle layer: before you enter or do something, you have to pass through it first. And Mongoose's Middleware turns out to be really handy. In this post I'll show you how to hook into events like save with pre and post.
Mongoose Static Methods: They Look Like Instance Methods, But They're Not

Next up is Static methods, which look a lot like Instance methods. But here's the catch — they're actually two different things. In this post I'll show you how to write a Static method, how it compares to an Instance method, and the one key difference that trips people up.
Clear LINE's Link Preview Cache With Page Poker

LINE is everywhere as a sharing channel, but once a link's been shared, LINE caches the preview aggressively — and there's no obvious way to bust the cache. Until you discover Page Poker. This post covers LINE's official Page Poker tool for clearing link preview cache, plus Facebook's Sharing Debugger as the equivalent for Facebook.
Mongoose Instance Methods: Add Your Own Methods to a Document

Mongoose ships with a ton of handy built-in methods, but sometimes your needs are a little special. When that happens, you can absolutely define your own. This post walks through Instance methods — how to attach custom methods to a Schema, why you should avoid arrow functions here, and how to use them with both findOne and find.
(16) The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango - The Weird Thing About Mongoose Update
The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango

Let's circle back to Update for a bit. There's actually something pretty weird about Update that I never covered, so I'm setting aside a whole post for it. If you've ever wondered why your Schema validation suddenly stops working when you update a document, this one's for you.
The Mango-Eating Frontend Engineer (15): Mongoose Delete

Time for the easiest chapter of the whole series — everything Delete-related. Let's see whether Mongoose's Delete is all that different from what you already know in MongoDB. Spoiler: it really isn't, but there are a couple of handy extras like findOneAndDelete that are worth knowing about.
Forced to Eat Mango (14): Updating Documents with Mongoose
Forced to Eat Mango as a Frontend Engineer

We've covered query syntax already, so now it's time to look at updates. Mongoose's update syntax is a little different from raw MongoDB, but honestly it's not hard at all. In this post I'll walk through updateOne, findOneAndUpdate, that handy new option, and why I personally reach for findOneAndUpdate most of the time.
Forced to Eat Mango (13): Reading Data with Mongoose

We already managed to Create a record, so the next obvious step is to Read it back — which in MongoDB land means finding data. Just keep one thing in mind: the syntax here is what Mongoose gives us, not raw MongoDB syntax. In this post I'll walk through find, findById, and how to add some fake data to play with.
(12) The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango - Mongoose Create and Model

We've already met Schema, SchemaType, and Schema Validators. But so far all we've done is tell Mongoose what type our data is — we haven't actually used any of it yet. In this post we finally put it to work: we'll turn a Schema into a Model and save our first document into MongoDB.
